Consumer Claim:
Background : My identity was unknowingly stolen by a previous roommate who lived in the same household sometime around XXXX. 

It just came to my attention that on XX/XX/XXXX the individual set up a 5 year contract with XXXX for a home alarm account with both of our names as the account holders ( maybe for co-sign purposes? ). This location of the residence where this alarm account was opened is on the opposite side of the country. 

The individual made payments for 1 year and 8 months before the account went delinquent. The account then went to collections a little while later, and landed in the hands of the debt collector named Perfection Collection around XX/XX/XXXX. 

When I was contacted by the debt collector, I let them know that I did not set up this account, nor have I ever lived in the state it was setup in, nor on that side of the United States. I let them know that the individual 's name on the account was a previous roommate, and that he must've stolen my identity 15+ years ago. 

They responded to my deny of the account by saying they already talked to the other individual and he said that I stole his information to setup the account, and then implied that they are only looking for repayment, not looking to get me in trouble for setting up the account under his name ; which isn't even true in the first place. 

I continued to get harassing calls for payment. When I asked how to fix the problem, they did not provide any assistance ; their only interest was to get repayment for the account, and did not take into consideration that the account was fraudulently setup in my name. 

I had to make several calls to get info on how to take this off my credit report, because they were not helping. When I finally reached someone, they were hesitant to give me the information about the account, as well as hesitant about telling me steps I need to get it resolved. 

I filed a police report with my local police, and found more information on steps to take from IdentityTheft.gov on my own ; however, when I call them now they no longer answer the phone, nor do they return my voicemails. 

The delinquent account is still on my credit report, and seems as though they are only wanting to work with me if I were to just pay it off this fraudulent account.

I also disputed this alleged debt with GC Services when I received their collection letter, dated XX/XX/XXXX, in the mail. I asked for the validation of the debt as required by Texas Debt Validation process on XX/XX/XXXX but XXXX XXXX never validated the debt but continued to pursued collection action and still reporting the debt on my credit record in violation of the FDCPA. 

Returned certified mail confirmation showed XXXX XXXX received my dispute letter but never responded and never validated the alleged debt but decided to misrepresent themselves to offset my {$3300.00} and take my money in Bad Faith. 

The US Dept. of Treasury had said that only the person named in 'who do I owe ' can be able to remove, change and refund the money collected through this falsified Treasury Offset process. But the US Dept of Treasury had failed to identify the real certifying agent of this Offset.

Therefore TOP Trace # : XXXX  was certified in violation of the Federal Debt collection Act and has to be nullified and my money return to me. 

I am also requesting that my information be removed from TOP data base in accordance with the Federal Debt Collection Act due to misrepresentation by XXXX XXXX, the certified agent.

Consumer Claim: Hello, I recently came to the conclusion of the scheduled payments of a {$55000.00} auto loan provided by Valley National Bank ( Acct : XXXX ) in XX/XX/2018. Upon receiving the final payoff statement, Valley National Bank incorporated over {$1000.00} of late payments and fees associated with payments which weren't made on the exact due date of the loan over the life of the loan. I purchased my vehicle at a dealership and had I known that the financing bank, Valley National Bank, didn't offer online payments I may have requested another lender ( if possible ), but nonetheless over the course of my loan term I admittedly didn't always submit a wire request from my primary bank in time to have the funds to Valley National by the due date. So upon receiving the final payoff statement from Valley National which included {$55.00} of principal and {$1000.00} of late fees, I simply asked Valley National to provide me with a reconciliation of the {$1000.00} of late fees over the course of five years of payments so I could validate those {$1000.00} of late fees and/or additional interest. After being bounced around various departments at Valley National I was told by a collections XXXX that the statement had to be mailed and couldn't be made available online. As I waited for the mailed copy to arrive, I continued to receive collection calls on the remaining principal balance and outstanding fees ( daily over the course of 2 weeks ). I assured the parties involved that I wasn't declining to pay and would make payment arrangements upon receipt of the mailed materials. Over the next 2 weeks all I received from Valley National in the mail was further notices of the remaining principal balance and requested fees. At this point I called Valley National collections to again ask on the status of the requested reconciliation materials. I was told that the materials request was never made and that another department would have to handle reconciliations. Concerned, I asked to speak to a manager and urged her to please initiate the request for the materials so I could reconcile. I even offered to make a payment at the time for the remaining principal balance while I awaited the reconciliation materials so as to close out the loan without any negative impact upon my credit status. She assured me that no further collection actions would be taken, no late payment report would be made to the credit bureaus and assured me that the materials would be mailed over the next few days. Ten days later, I still had not received the materials in the mail and again called the company, at which point I was able to " get to a person '' who had working knowledge of the fee reconciliation system. Rather than asking yet again for the reconciliation to be mailed, I just had her verbally read the day count and $ fees associated with the various payments which weren't made on the due date over the course of the loan, at which point I agreed to pay the principal balance and fees in full, fully expecting that I would never receive a reconciliation in the mail. Days later I received the vehicle title in the mail and figured this was closure, however upon application for a refinance of my existing home loan ( XX/XX/XXXX ) I now discover that over the course of this whole ordeal, Valley National reported me as 30 days late two times for the unpaid {$55.00} balance, while all the while I was simply waiting for their reconciliation of late fees on the payoff notice to be provided to me. I contacted Valley National and asked them to please restate the report to the credit bureau given that my delays were related to an ask for a simple reconciliation of late fees over the course of a five year loan. Valley National declined to consider this and says " what is done is done ''. Is there any remediation that can be taken at this point. This is virtually the only blemish on my credit over a period of 20+ years and is now impacting the cost of my home loan refinancing. This feels like a classic case of poor communication between departments within a loan servicer, please help ...

This does not meet the Federal Trade Commissions guidelines of what constitutes proper debt validation In addition, in the case of XXXX Vs. XXXX ( IN THE COURT OF APPEALS OF INDIANA, No. XXXX ), the court ruled that : XXXX5 U.S.C. 1692g ( b ) ( emphasis added ). On XX/XX/XXXX, nineteen days after the date of XXXX debt collection letter, XXXX counsel XXXX sent XXXX a letter declaring that XXXX disputes your debt collection-related allegations, denies the same, and demands strict proof and verification thereof. Record at 21. As such, XXXX should have ceased his debt collection efforts immediately upon receiving that letter. Instead, XXXX proceeded to obtain a default judgment against XXXX on the debt collection claim before he had mailed XXXX the necessary verification and, thus, violated 15 U.S.C. 1692g ( b ). 

XXXX maintains, however, that there was no violation of the FDCPA because he sent adequate verification of the debt [ to XXXX ] in the XX/XX/XXXX notice of claim. Brief of Appellee at 13. Specifically, XXXX claims that a copy of the consumer credit contract between XXXX and American General attached to the notice of claim provided sufficient verification of the debt within the meaning of 15 U.S.C. 1692g ( b ). We can not agree. 

The contract in no way provides sufficient verification of the debt. A review of the document reveals that it identifies only the terms of XXXX loan, including a 17.99 % annual interest rate and the original loan amount of {$2500.00}. The loan agreement contains no accounting of any payments made by XXXX, the dates on which those payments were made, the interest which had accrued, or any late fees which had been assessed once XXXX stopped making the required payments. Indeed, the existing unpaid contract balance at the time XXXX sent the debt collection notice was at least {$350.00} more than the original loan amount. Therefore, XXXX violated 15 U.S.C. 1692g ( b ) when he failed to cease collection of the debt by obtaining a default judgment against XXXX after XXXX had notified XXXX in writing that he was disputing the debt but before XXXX had mailed verification of the debt to XXXX. We reverse the trial courts entry of summary judgment in favor of XXXX on this issue. 

I notice that you are still reporting this collection on my credit report, which is considered to be a collection activity. I was told I was denied, because I had to be a customer for at least 60 days before I could apply for a credit line increase. So now I have a credit card that is basically useless to me and an inquiry on my credit report. 

If I am applying for a line of credit, I should have all the information up front so I can make a decision on whether or not I want to move forward with the application or if the card is right for me. Obviously this card was not the right fit for my based off of the information I found out after I applied for it.

On XX/XX/2019, we noticed unauthorized activity on our account. 

We called the bank at XXXX and asked for a freeze to be placed on the account. The representative on the telephone said he could not do this because he could not ID the caller, even though we provided all the information he requested. This was the first of a series of bizarre and incompetent actions by the bank. 

Since the telephone rep was useless, we rushed to the bank at XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX, XXXX XXXX, FL XXXX, in person and were assisted by associate XXXX XXXX. The unauthorized activity reported was three online transfers from some money service called XXXX which we have never heard of and never used, in the amounts of {$50.00}, {$300.00} and {$500.00} totalling {$850.00}. 

The associate at first appeared to be helpful, stating not to worry at all, the good news is you always get your money back. We followed instructions carefully, the associate stated that this was a known issue with customer data stolen from cellular telephones serviced by XXXX. She placed phone calls in our presence and we were told that the issue would be resolved in about 10 days. Again, we requested that the account be frozen, and I was told that this would not be necessary, that the issue was a pretty standard claim that would be resolved easily. 

On XX/XX/2019, while waiting for a resolution, we noticed more unauthorized activity someone was trying to connect an unknown XXXX account to our bank account and made verification deposits and withdrawals. This attempt was denied by XXXX, but we again went to the same bank branch and reported the issue. This time we were served by XXXX XXXX, and this is where things got even more unbelievable. The associate said OK, let 's have your telephone and we'll call claims. We didn't understand and asked for clarification. The employee explained that it would be faster to call from a customer 's cellular telephone than to go through the main system. THIS IS LUDICROUS. We were visiting a bank branch because we had been victims of fraud possibly originating from cellular telephone hacks and we were asked to use our possibly compromised telephones rather than the bank 's which would be assumed to be more secure! We denied this and used the bank 's telephones! When Claims were contacted by the associate, they stated that they could not find a record of a fraud claim ( which was done on our first visit on XX/XX/2019 ) and that a new claim would have to be made for all the unauthorized transactions. A new claim was made. This time, a number was provided XXXX. 

I SPECIFICALLY ASKED THE ASSOCIATE why they could not just cancel or deny the unauthorized transactions, which were still pending, and she said once they are initiated, they can not be stopped. This is also absolutely untrue! 

Sensing that something was wrong, we trusted out instinct and withdrew the bulk amount of the money in the account in the form of a cashier 's check. We left a small amount of money in the account ( {$230.00} ) to cover some legitimate payments which had already been initiated PRIOR to the fraud.

We were, by now in complete shock at this TOTAL CHAOS. 

XXXX was able to contact Fraud Operations who placed a HOLD on the account in the form of negative XXXX ( XXXX ), again we were told not to panic, the issue would be resolved in 10 days. 

The branch manager XXXX told us WE DON'T DO FRAUD HERE, THIS IS A BANK BRANCH THAT ONLY DEALS IN EVERYDAY TRANSACTIONS and DON'T WORRY, THESE THINGS HAPPEN ALL THE TIME!!! Very comforting indeed!
